name: Ma Diao (馬吊) — Chinese Money-Suited Cards (the Deep Root)
description:
# Ma Diao (馬吊) — Chinese money-suited cards

## At a glance

The **deepest root of the playing-card form itself** — and, deliberately, *not* a direct ancestor of tarot. Paper playing cards are usually traced to **Tang-dynasty China** (618–907 CE) — though the famous 9th-c. 'leaf game' reference is disputed (it may describe a book or board game), and the earliest *unambiguous* paper playing cards are Yuan-era, c. 1294; by the time of the Song and Ming they had developed into **money-suited** packs whose suits are denominations of currency. **Ma Diao** (馬吊, 'hanging horse') is the best-documented of these games, codified in the **late Ming** (16th–17th c.) and described in period manuals. This library includes it to show where the *idea* of a suited pack of cards begins, several removes upstream of anything European.

## The money suits

Four ascending money denominations: **cash → strings of cash → myriads → tens-of-myriads** (文 · 索 · 萬 · 十萬). The suits are not abstract pips but units of money — the structural signature of the East-Asian tradition. The **myriad (萬) suit** was, in many sets, illustrated with the 108 bandit-heroes of the novel *Water Margin* (水滸傳), so painted 'Water Margin cards' (水滸牌) are a famous money-suited variety still made in folk workshops today.

## Relationship to tarot — a deep root, not a parent

The standard scholarly thesis (Wilkinson, Needham, Lo) is that the playing-card *form* diffused **westward out of China**, reaching Europe through the **Islamic world**, where the **Mamluk pack** (Egypt/Syria) is the deck Europe directly copied. So the line to tarot runs **China → (centuries, many intermediaries) → Mamluk → Italian/Spanish suits → trionfi/tarot**. Ma Diao is a **cousin and a deep root**, like Ganjifa — it shows the origin of the form, but no card here descends by a clean line into a tarot trump. The detail of the transmission is genuinely debated; what is not debated is that paper cards are a Chinese invention.

## Game, never divination

Ma Diao is a **trick-and-draw gambling game** for four players, played with a 40-card money-suited pack. There is no cartomantic tradition attached to it — it sits in this library purely as the upstream of the *object*, not of any reading practice.

## Provenance & evidence

The cards shown are the **public-domain holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden** (high-resolution museum scans on Wikimedia Commons), a European collection of an East-Asian pack. Twelve items survive there. Individual suit/value identification is left open rather than guessed.

## Sources

- W. H. Wilkinson, 'Chinese Origin of Playing Cards' (1895); Joseph Needham, *Science and Civilisation in China*, vol. 5; Andrew Lo on Ma Diao and Water Margin cards; Dummett & Mann, *The Game of Tarot* (on the China→Islam→Europe diffusion). Card images: Skokloster Castle / Wikimedia Commons, public domain.

---
*AI-assisted first-pass draft in the house evidence-first style — claims hedged, transmission flagged as debated. Corrections welcome.*
_source: Wikimedia Commons (Skokloster Castle), public domain. Hotlinked with attribution.

sections.What it is: The lowest money suit: single copper coins (wén). The coin is the ancestor of the European 'Coins/Pentacles' suit — the one suit that survived the long diffusion west more or less recognisably.
Strings or ropes (suǒ) on which coins were threaded — each string a bundle of one hundred cash. A denomination, not an abstract pip: the suits of this pack are literally units of money.

Ten-thousand-cash units (wàn). In many money-card sets this suit was illustrated with the 108 bandit-heroes of the novel *Water Margin* (水滸傳) — so 'Water Margin cards' (水滸牌) are a famous money-suited variety.
The top suit (the 'decade' / tens-of-myriads), carrying the honour cards. The four ascending money denominations — cash → strings → myriads → tens-of-myriads — are the whole structure of the game.

Ma Diao is a *money-suited* pack: its four suits are ascending denominations of cash, not the cups/swords/coins/batons of the Mamluk and European line. This is the structural signature of the East-Asian card tradition.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.

sections.What it is: The twelve Ma Diao items that survive in Sweden at Skokloster Castle — a fragmentary but high-resolution, openly public-domain witness to a money-suited Chinese pack.
